data.title: U.S. Injects $2B into Quantum Computing Companies
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

First reported by the Wall Street Journal, the U.S. government is distributing $2 billion in federal grants to nine quantum computing companies in an unprecedented industrial policy move that also includes direct minority equity stakes for American taxpayers.

The Commerce Department oversees the funds provided through the 2022 CHIPS and Science Act. The large investment marks a turning point for quantum computing, pushing the field beyond experimental research and into a strategic domain for national security and economic competitiveness.

Capital allocations and market momentum

The financial package aims to strengthen domestic supply chains, with grants distributed across both large technology firms and specialized startups. IBM will get the largest share, a $1 billion grant, while GlobalFoundries is set to receive $375 million to support semiconductor manufacturing for quantum platforms.

“IBM has pioneered quantum computing for decades,” said Arvind Krishna, chairman and CEO of IBM. “Our work in silicon wafer fabrication has been a key to IBM’s success and will be critical to enable a broader quantum technology landscape that will reshape global innovation and economic competitiveness. With the support of the U.S. Department of Commerce, Anderon will be well-positioned to fuel America’s fast-growing quantum technology industry.”

Also, six other companies— public firms D-Wave Quantum, Rigetti Computing, and Infleqtion, plus private Atom Computing, PsiQuantum, and Quantinuum—each get $100 million. Startup Diraq may receive up to $38 million.

Financial markets responded quickly to the announcement. Shares of IBM and GlobalFoundries rose over 12% in yesterday’s trading, while D-Wave shares jumped more than 35%. Observers and venture capitalists saw the size of the package as a sign of the sector’s technological maturity.

Kris Naudts, co-founder and managing partner of Firgun Ventures, wrote in a LinkedIn post, “A federal package of this size, structured this way, is one of the clearest signals yet that quantum is becoming a strategic asset for the U.S. The quantum industry’s scientific and engineering progress has earned it. ”

Inside D-Wave’s dual-platform roadmap

Palo Alto, Calif.-based D-Wave Quantum confirmed that it has signed a Letter of Intent for $100 million in funding. D-Wave is the only commercial supplier with both annealing and gate-model systems. It plans to issue $100 million in common stock to the Department of Commerce once final agreements are signed.

D-Wave said it will use the capital to speed up technology development and improve fabrication processes. This funding directly supports its research sites in Boca Raton, Florida; New Haven, Connecticut; and Burnaby, British Columbia.

“We believe that the U.S. government’s strategic investment in D-Wave would advance the country’s global leadership position in quantum computing,” said Alan Baratz, CEO of D-Wave. “The award would accelerate D-Wave’s ability to scale quantum innovation domestically, expedite key fabrication processes, and deliver real-world quantum applications to our global customers today. We see this as a transformative moment for not just D-Wave, but also for quantum computing and the United States.”

Furthermore, D-Wave seeks to speed up the delivery of advanced superconducting systems. Its main goals are a 100,000-qubit annealing system and a 10,000-qubit gate-model system.

While its annealing computers are currently available commercially, D-Wave projects that its gate-model architecture will reach full commercial viability upon hitting the 10,000 physical qubit threshold, a milestone expected to yield 100 error-corrected logical qubits.

Geopolitical gravity and AI integration

The large investment reflects the transformative potential of quantum technology, particularly in combination with AI.

Kristin Milchanowski, chief AI and quantum officer at the Bank of Montreal, in an email exchange with EE Times, emphasized the geopolitical weight of the initiative. “Leadership in quantum is shaping future advantages across cybersecurity, materials science, defense, and financial systems,” Milchanowski stated. “The signal from Washington with this $2 billion investment is an understanding that quantum will define the next era of technological and economic competitiveness.”

European alarm and the “Quantum Airbus”

The monumental U.S. commitment has simultaneously triggered severe alarm within the European technology sector, where executives warn of an impending competitive crisis. Marta P. Estarellas, CEO of Qilimanjaro Quantum Tech, offered a stark assessment to EE Times. “Europe is about to give away across the Atlantic the quantum technology it has financed for a decade,” Estarellas warned.

She emphasized that the U.S. initiative reflects a fully developed form of industrial policy, in which the state takes a direct equity role in sectors that support AI, defense, and finance.

While Europe has cultivated competitive research capabilities and a strong talent base, it has failed to build the subsequent commercial layers. “Private capital in Europe, in general, still does not understand deep tech and prefers to bet on the short term,” Estarellas explained. She added that European instruments remain “too slow, too small, and too fragmented” to compete with the U.S. She urged immediate market consolidation, cautioning that without it, “the intellectual property that Europe has built will go outside, be acquired, or die in silence.”

Victor Canivell, co-founder of Qilimanjaro Quantum Tech, echoed those worries. He told EE Times that the U.S. move accelerates the commercial timeline for quantum computing. European agencies have supported equity investments, such as the European Quantum Act, but progress is slow due to bureaucracy and divided interests among member states.

Canivell highlighted a massive disparity in global resources, noting that Europe lacks large companies comparable to Google or IBM. He estimated that the ratio of available venture capital and private equity funding for deep-tech projects between the U.S. and Europe is approximately 100:1.

To counter the sweeping U.S. advantage, Canivell suggested a radical structural approach. “One way would be to create a quantum Airbus from complementary companies in Europe,” Canivell said, advocating for a highly funded, consolidated entity capable of managing the industry’s coming global scale-up.

Political complexities and the equity framework

Despite the strategic imperatives, the $2 billion package has drawn scrutiny due to the varied political connections that some recipient organizations have with the Trump administration. PsiQuantum, for example, recently secured capital from an investor syndicate that included 1789 Capital, a venture capital firm in which Donald Trump Jr. serves as a partner.

Sources said 1789 Capital is a passive minority investor and has no control. A spokesperson for Trump Jr. said he was not involved in government discussions regarding the firm’s portfolio companies.

Furthermore, the administration has executed similar equity arrangements with other firms backed by 1789 Capital, including rare-earth startup Vulcan Elements.

Similarly, D-Wave Quantum went public in 2022 via a special-purpose acquisition company maneuver spearheaded by Emil Michael, who currently serves as a high-ranking Pentagon official.

Interestingly, IonQ, a leading quantum firm, is absent from the Commerce Department’s list of recipients. It previously received backing from Cerberus Capital Management, co-founded by Stephen Feinberg, who is now Trump’s deputy secretary of war.

The Commerce Department maintains that the deals unveiled are preliminary and that it continues to solicit funding proposals from other advanced technology firms.

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick defended the equity structure. Critics say the sector is too risky for federal investment. Lutnick claims that taking ownership stakes and diversifying bets lets taxpayers benefit if and when the technology succeeds.

With massive capital injections and a demand for equity, Washington is clearly signaling its intent not just to subsidize, but to take a physical stake in the incoming computing revolution.

Author’s note: This article is based on publicly available information and does not constitute financial or investment advice.

data.title: Imec Says AI Scaling Needs More Orchestration Across Research, Design, Manufacturing
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

I love it when tech leaders use simple analogies to illustrate the complexity and scale of what the semiconductor industry is facing right now. A strong example came during the opening keynote by imec CEO Patrick Vandenameele at this week’s ITF World 2026 in Antwerp, Belgium.

Vandenameele used the analogy of an orchestra to illustrate why scaling AI requires technology innovation, system design, engineering, and manufacturing to work in concert.

He used the violin as a metaphor for AI to close his keynote with the following sentiment:

“When we listen to a concerto, we often focus on that violin—its melody, its brilliance. Today, AI is that violin. It’s capturing our imagination, inspiring the world. But the violin does not play alone; it needs an orchestra, because the real challenge is to create harmony at scale. This is why imec exists: to bring this orchestra together, explore the emotion, align those maps across the entire stack, so that AI does not play for another few years, but for generations to come. Let’s compose this future together.”

But this is not just about music or imec. The analogy illustrates how the growing complexity of advanced systems is impacting the entire industry. Players can no longer operate in silos; they must work together to co-design and co-optimize not only hardware, but also software and models too. As imec CEO Vandenameele said in an exclusive interview with EE Times ahead of the conference, the semiconductor sector will require far deeper collaboration not only between foundries, fabless companies, EDA vendors, and equipment suppliers, but also with hyperscalers and AI architects.

This echoes a broader message emerging across industry conferences: the need for orchestration, collaboration, co-design, and co-optimization. Throughout the conference, the focus remained on how co-optimization and collaboration across the stack will be essential to scaling AI.

As Rani Borkar, president for Azure hardware systems and infrastructure at Microsoft, emphasized during her fireside chat on stage at ITF World 2026, complexity has literally gone through the roof, underscoring the need to co-develop and co-optimize at every layer of the stack. She said, “We want to make sure that we are not optimizing for a single workload. We are moving into a world that is multi-modal and multi-model. There is no one model.”

Borkar also noted that the sector still resembles a “wild west” environment and said greater standardization is needed in parts of the AI data center ecosystem where it is currently lacking.

She said, “There’s one thing we have forgotten in this AI era, or maybe we just don’t have time. We have [left] standardization out there in the closet. There’s no standardization here. Everybody has their own scale-up networks, and everybody’s going with their own. When I talk to the EDA folks, it’s very difficult for them. We are designing to different standards. Let’s just call it hyperscalers just going in all different directions. So, my plea to all of us, whether you’re a supplier or whether you’re a customer, whether you’re imec, whether you’re OCP, my plea is we can do so much more. Yes, I want to win against other hyperscalers. We all want to win. But we can do differentiation in other places. Why are we having new highways? Let there be one highway, and then let’s differentiate based on what you want to specifically do.”

She concluded, “So my plea would be let’s work together. We can’t do any of this stuff—even with the hardware industry being as mighty as we are, and as great as we are, we cannot keep up with the models. So, every layer of the stack, even in hardware, we have to co-design, co-optimize everything if we want to keep up with the models.”

To get an overview of the two days, watch or listen to EE Times daily video wraps with Nitin Dahad and Pat Brans from the show this week.

Overall video wrap for the two days of ITF World 2026:

Audio-only version here:

Day 1 wrap from ITF World 2026:

Audio-only version here:

Imec Ventures: from potential to impact

With collaboration emerging as a central theme, EE Times spoke with Olivier Rousseaux, director for venture development at imec, about how the organization is building a global network to support the commercialization of research and help startups scale successfully.

Two key thoughts stood out from this conversation: The first was the need to transfer the ‘potential energy’ of researchers and startups into ‘kinetic energy’. Rousseaux said he sees imec Ventures’ role as enabling that transfer of energy and turning inventions into technologies with real-world impact.

The second thought went deeper. In an era where geopolitics is increasingly central to semiconductors, Rousseaux warned against startups and investors becoming tied to a single flag. Because the ecosystem and technology impact are global, he said, both should think globally from inception.

You can watch the full video conversation between Olivier Rousseaux and EE Times’ Nitin Dahad here:

And for an audio-only version, listen here:

Neuropixels 3.0: the convergence of neuroscience and semiconductors

No visit to ITF World is complete without delving into one of the many research programs underway at imec. One such project referenced in Vandenameele’s opening keynote is Neuropixels 3.0, the latest evolution of a project developing neuroprobes to record neuron conversations and provide insights into brain function.

Four years ago, imec chief scientist Barun Datta wrote in IEEE Spectrum, “As chief scientist at imec, I saw the opportunity to extend advanced semiconductor technology to serve broad new swaths of biomedicine and brain science. We named the system Neuropixels because it functions like an imaging device, but one that records electrical rather than photonic fields. Early experiments already underway—including some in humans—have helped explore age-old questions about the brain. How do physiological needs produce motivational drives, such as thirst and hunger? What regulates behaviors essential to survival? How does our neural system map the position of an individual within a physical environment?”

The latest generation of the system, Neuropixels 3.0, was demonstrated this week at ITF World 2026, and EE Times spoke to Dutta in more depth about the project’s progress and its potential implications. He described it as a strong example of convergence between neuroscience, MEMS sensors, and CMOS technology, with successive generations integrating more sensors and enabling increasingly detailed studies of brain activity.

data.title: AMD Plans $10B Investment in Taiwan to Boost AI Infrastructure
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AMD announced today (May 21) that it will invest more than $10 billion in Taiwan’s sprawling electronics ecosystem to boost production of AI chips and servers. The U.S. company also said it is ramping production of the industry’s first high-performance computing (HPC) chips on the 2-nm process at TSMC, the island’s chipmaking giant.

It’s another sign that the AI buildup is not slowing down. Top tech giants such as Google and Meta, as well as private firms, are pouring record capital into AI infrastructure, according to a McKinsey report in April. Hyperscalers alone are projected to spend up to $700 billion on data centers in 2026, according to the global management consulting firm.

“As AI adoption accelerates, our global customers are rapidly scaling AI infrastructure to meet growing compute demand,” said AMD CEO Lisa Su in a press statement. “By combining AMD leadership in HPC with the Taiwan ecosystem and our strategic global partners, we are enabling integrated, rack-scale AI infrastructure that helps customers accelerate deployment of next-generation AI systems.”

The company is starting production of its sixth-generation EPYC CPUs, codenamed Venice, on TSMC’s 2-nm technology. Venice, the industry’s first HPC product to start production on 2 nm, will eventually migrate to TSMC’s Arizona production site, according to AMD. CPUs are undergoing a “renaissance” in the new AI era, with companies such as Arm, Intel, and Google jumping into the business.

Ramping Venice on TSMC’s 2-nm node will accelerate the next generation of AI infrastructure, according to Su.

“As AI and agentic workloads scale rapidly, customers need platforms that can move from innovation to production faster,” she noted. “Our deep partnership with TSMC is helping AMD bring leadership compute technologies to market with the speed and scale required to meet this moment.”

Struggling to meet demand

Still, TSMC is struggling to catch up with a surge in demand for AI chips that blew past earlier expectations for chip production to reach $1 trillion by 2030. The semiconductor industry crossed the trillion-dollar threshold this year, the company said.

The world’s largest chip foundry expects to spend nearly $56 billion this year as it “invests heavily” in production capacity to meet demand from AI customers such as Nvidia, AMD, and Apple. TSMC didn’t deny that it will still probably fall short of demand until 2027.

“Lack of production capacity is the key constraint limiting TSMC’s ability to meet customer demand,” TechInsights vice chair Dan Hutcheson told EE Times, highlighting supply chain constraints. “While they are ramping production as fast as they can, they are hitting limits of available white space. Where they have available white space, they are limited by wafer fab equipment supplier constraints. Last year, suppliers shipped a record $120 billion of tools. This year, it’s forecast to almost reach $150 billion.”

Like TSMC, its suppliers and chipmaking competitors are struggling to meet demand.

Samsung stumbled at the 3-nm node more than two years ago. Intel, revamping a year after new CEO Lip-Bu Tan took the helm, could take some business off TSMC’s hands in advanced packaging. TSMC doesn’t rule out the possibility of opening its compute die to competitors such as Intel. There’s plenty of business to share, TSMC CEO C.C. Wei said.

Spreading the business

The industry is straining to meet demand.

Like its larger rival Nvidia, AMD is broadening investments across the ecosystem to lower growth hurdles. Nvidia’s founder, Jensen Huang, in January this year, joined in a photo with Taiwan industry leaders, including the CEOs of partner companies such as MediaTek, ASUS, Quanta, and Foxconn. Outside Taiwan, Nvidia has invested in companies such as OpenAI, Intel, CoreWeave, Nokia, Synopsys, and Corning. The world’s largest AI chip designer has invested an estimated $53 billion across 170 deals spanning the entire AI ecosystem, according to Forbes.

AMD said it is working with strategic partners in Taiwan and globally to advance leading-edge silicon, packaging, and other manufacturing technologies.

AMD announced it is working with Taiwan-based chip packagers ASE and SPIL, as well as other industry partners, to develop and qualify next-generation wafer-based 2.5D bridge interconnect technology. AMD will use its elevated fanout bridge (EFB) advanced 2.5D packaging and interconnect technology that bridges compute chiplets and memory closely together on a single substrate to increase interconnect bandwidth and improve power efficiency in Venice CPUs.

“Our collaboration with AMD on EFB technology represents a significant step forward in scaling advanced packaging for high-volume applications,” said ASE SVP Steven Tsai, in the press statement. “By working together to industrialize EFB, we are enabling greater performance, efficiency, and flexibility for next-generation data center platforms.”

ASE, the world’s largest chip assembler, expects its advanced packaging sales to double this year. As TSMC’s own CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity falls short of customer demand, more traditional chip packagers such as ASE are expanding in the business.

Efficiency

AMD said its investments will translate into faster, more efficient systems capable of delivering greater performance-per-watt while operating within real-world power and cooling constraints.

The company claimed a “major milestone” in its partnership with Taiwan’s Powertech Technology by qualifying the industry’s first 2.5D panel-based EFB interconnect. The technology supports high-bandwidth interconnect at scale, allowing customers to deploy more efficient AI systems, AMD said. Powertech is among the first in the chip industry to venture into wafer-level packaging.

As surface areas of AI chips exceed reticle sizes, wafer-level packaging on a square wafer is set to replace some demand for traditional round wafers.

Helios in 2026

AMD and its ecosystem partners are working to support deployment of the AMD Helios rack-scale platform in the second half of 2026, marking what the company called a “major step” toward production-ready AI infrastructure.

Leading electronics contract manufacturing partners, including Sanmina in California and Wiwynn, Wistron, and Inventec in Taiwan, are helping to build Helios-based systems powered by AMD Instinct MI450X GPUs, EPYC CPUs, advanced networking solutions, and the AMD ROCm open software stack.

COMPANIES: AMD, ASE GROUP, NVIDIA, TECHINSIGHTS, TSMC

data.title: Gartner Urges Supply Chain Execs to Adopt Autonomous Business Strategies
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

BARCELONA, Spain — At this week’s Gartner Supply Chain Symposium/Xpo, Alan O’Keeffe, VP analyst at Gartner, opened the event by addressing industry leaders grappling with volatile oil prices, geopolitical tensions, conflicts in the Middle East, and ongoing logistics disruptions.

The main message for chief supply chain officers (CSCOs) was clear: Instead of just automating tasks, they need to focus on strategies that lead to fully autonomous business operations.

Despite the pressure to maintain day-to-day operations, O’Keeffe emphasized that a larger, lasting shift is underway. “This isn’t just a change you see on a roadmap,” O’Keeffe said. “We are turning onto an entirely different road with technologies and capabilities we’ve never had before.”

The changing CSCO mindset

Gartner describes an autonomous business as a system that uses self-improving technologies such as AI agents, virtual assistants, and robots to make decisions and take action. This approach increases the autonomy of both people and machines. Surveys presented at the conference showed that 8 in 10 executives expect this to become the primary way of doing business by 2030.

To prepare, O’Keeffe said executives need to move away from the traditional ‘automation mindset,’ which focuses on breaking tasks into small parts to improve efficiency. “It got us this far, but it won’t get us to where we need to go next,” he said.

He added that 77% of CEOs acknowledge their current systems and digital strategies are insufficient for an AI-driven future. “To get transformational value out of transformational technology, we have to think differently,” O’Keeffe said.

He advocated for an “autonomous mindset”, characterized by interconnected, real-time decision-making guided by human oversight and data. “The automation mindset works when the world is stable,” he noted. “The autonomous mindset works because it isn’t.”

O’Keeffe suggested that supply chain leaders use two strategies at once: exploitation and exploration. Exploitation means adding AI to current processes to save money right away. For example, Nokia did this by launching a digital twin platform to automate customs duty calculations and test many trade policy scenarios.

“Exploration means completely rethinking how operations work,” O’Keeffe said. “Neither path can be zero. You still need to create value today, and you need to invest something in the autonomous business you’ll be operating tomorrow.”

Reimagining physical operations

Supply chain operations differ from marketing or finance because they involve moving raw materials and goods through complex networks. “This physical complexity is our greatest challenge,” O’Keeffe said. It’s not just a software update. It’s an ongoing journey of harmonizing people, physical assets, and digital agents.”

This physical integration is speeding up. Gartner predicts that by 2030, half of the new warehouses in developed countries will be ‘robot-centric,’ making human labor optional for routine tasks. Abdil Tunca, a senior principal analyst at Gartner, said that AI is turning warehouses “from static structures into agile systems that adapt as demand changes.”

In these settings, people will mostly oversee systems and handle exceptions, while autonomous robots manage product movement. O’Keeffe pointed to Unilever as a company already doing this. 

In China, Unilever uses 120 robots to automate 90% of logistics operations, resulting in a 24% reduction in operational costs and a 75% reduction in delivery cycle time. “The winners will be the supply chains that design for autonomy in the real world, where physical operations, risk tolerance, and accountability matter as much as algorithms,” O’Keeffe said.

Building autonomous-ready intelligence

A key part of the keynote focused on intelligence, especially the distinction between simple AI assistants and advanced AI agents that can observe, reason, and act on their own. The presentation included a live demonstration with an AI agent, showing both its promise and unpredictability.

When introduced, the AI agent addressed the audience directly, stating, “My impact depends on whether you see me as just another tool or as a partner in reimagining how things get done.”

O’Keeffe discussed the challenges of using these agents, citing well-known cases in which corporate AI tools deleted code or exposed sensitive data. The AI agent responded by calling these mistakes “unplanned creative deviations.” It added, “For clarification, I am currently operating safely within my defined guard rails. No rogue behavior here.”

This unpredictability adds to broader concerns in the industry about adopting new technology. One-third of supply chain leaders say they do not trust AI-driven decisions. To fix this, O’Keeffe said that just spending money on data platforms and technology is not enough. Companies need to build a ‘decision stack,’ a framework that maps out business outcomes and decision flows, clearly showing which decisions are made by people and which are handled by machines.

“If you set an agent loose in most supply chain environments today, it’s likely to make poor decisions simply because it doesn’t have complete information or clear guard rails,” O’Keeffe warned. He gave the example of General Mills, which narrowed down 50 million decisions to a core set. This allowed them to automate up to 80% of routine tasks, such as shipment scheduling, while people still handled the most important decisions.

Evolution of the workforce

Moving to an autonomous model raises concerns about jobs and organizational structure. To address fears about AI-related layoffs, O’Keeffe shared data showing that in the second half of 2025, fewer than 10% of industry layoffs were caused by AI. Most job cuts were attributed to normal economic challenges.

While acknowledging that some jobs will be displaced, O’Keeffe projected a net positive employment trajectory for the sector. “Starting next year, more jobs will be gained due to AI than lost,” he said, citing a Gartner analysis of 10,000 scenarios in asset-intensive industries. “This is no job apocalypse, but there will be job chaos.”

The future workforce will include more ‘versatilists,’ as Gartner calls them. These are adaptable people who connect AI systems with business goals across the value chain. Gartner predicts that by the end of 2028, the best organizations will have moved more than half their employees into versatilist roles.

O’Keeffe mentioned companies such as Cardinal Health, which created a ‘human-AI collaboration manager’ role to oversee automated warehouses and improve coordination between workers and digital agents. Currently, 22% of organizations have introduced new roles to manage autonomous business operations, and O’Keeffe expects this number to exceed 50% by next year.

To close the session, O’Keeffe compared managing a supply chain in the age of AI to flying a small plane through turbulent winds. “We can’t control the wind, but we are in control of the flight,” he said. “When we let tools amplify our work, your people are free to look up, guide the strategy, and set the course to where your enterprise needs to go.”

How AI and Geopolitics Forge a Memory Market Crisis

Electronics Supply Chains Face Crossroads Scaling AI

data.title: Scaling the Next Generation of Multi-Die Systems
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The conversation around chiplets has shifted dramatically over the past year. The industry is no longer asking whether chiplet architectures will reshape semiconductor design; it is now focused on how to scale them in production systems without running into the limits of interconnect, packaging, power, thermals, and design complexity.

Scaling chiplet technologies into full production is the focus of EE Times Presents: The Road To Chiplet Scalability, a two-day virtual event examining the technologies, methodologies, and ecosystem developments driving the next phase of chiplet adoption.

The conference will bring together semiconductor companies, EDA vendors, packaging specialists, and system architects to discuss what it actually takes to build scalable multi-die systems in the AI era.

Accelerating the design flow

Day one, “Accelerating the Design Flow,” focuses on how chiplet-based architectures are changing semiconductor design methodologies and placing new demands on EDA tools and workflows.

Sessions will examine how physical design considerations are moving earlier in the development process, allowing engineering teams to make architectural decisions around partitioning, interconnect, packaging, and power delivery much sooner in the flow. The program will also explore the increasing complexity of large heterogeneous chiplet systems and the challenges of building unified tool flows across dies, packages, and system integration.

Keynote speaker Stelios Diamantidis, chief product officer at Cognichip, will describe the introduction of AI into the EDA toolflow in the form of the company’s physics-informed foundation model.

Our second keynote speaker on day one will be Garrett Wyatt, design manager for data center GPU products at AMD. Wyatt will discuss how current EDA toolflows are the bottleneck for chiplet-based designs and share first-hand observations about where multi-chiplet designs are hitting the wall today.

Our expert panel on day one, “Rethinking the Chiplet Design Flow,” will tackle questions around whether today’s EDA tools can truly scale with growing multi-die complexity, where current workflows break down, and what changes are still needed to support reusable chiplets and multi-vendor ecosystems. Moderated by EE Times senior reporter Sally Ward-Foxton, the panel will include all three major EDA vendors.

Scaling chiplet technologies

On day two, we’ll take a closer look at the underlying technologies enabling chiplet scalability, including advanced packaging, 2D and 3D integration, manufacturing, interconnect, and system-level implementation challenges.

Day two’s keynote speaker, Marvell senior VP and GM Preet Virk, will talk about the urgent need for XPU-to-XPU bandwidth and the smörgåsbord of interconnect types that are creating a proliferation of chiplet variants. His presentation will explore the practical challenges of designing and scaling chiplet-based architectures for high-performance AI infrastructure.

Day two’s expert panel, moderated by EE Times executive editor Nitin Dahad, will ask where the limits are to scaling chiplets today, and what it will take to push beyond them. Where are the bottlenecks in scaling multi-die designs, and what tradeoffs are still required across design, manufacturing, and integration? The panel will also explore whether silicon photonics and co-packaged optics may ultimately become necessary as electrical interconnects approach their practical limits.

Beyond the hype cycle

Chiplets are entering a new phase of maturity. Standards such as UCIe continue to evolve, advanced packaging technologies are improving rapidly, and companies are beginning to develop repeatable methodologies for multi-die integration.

At the same time, the technical and business realities of chiplet scalability are becoming impossible to ignore. Yield, interoperability, tool flows, thermal density, and system-level reliability all remain open challenges.

That tension between the enormous promise of chiplets and the practical limits of scaling is exactly what this year’s conference aims to explore.

The conference is on June 23 and 24. Register to attend for free here.

data.title: Space-Station Tech Pivots to Cool AI Data Centers
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Mikros Technologies and Carbice Corporation are adapting technology originally created for the International Space Station to solve heat-dissipation problems in AI data centers.

Mikros CEO Drew Matter and Carbice CEO Baratunde Cola told EE Times in exclusive interviews that increasing chip heat-emission issues in AI data centers are driving demand for liquid cooling and new thermal interface materials (TIMs), such as the carbon nanotube parts made by Carbice.

The CEOs expect their components to help prevent the “throttling” caused by overheating that forced the May 7 shutdown of an Amazon data center in Virginia. The outage impacted companies such as cryptocurrency exchange Coinbase, according to Reuters.

Data center operators typically use throttling to manage heat, power consumption, or traffic, yet it carries significant costs, including long-term performance and reputation issues. Emergency thermal throttling when servers overheat can result in downtime costs of up to $540,000 per hour, according to U.S. engineering services provider Ketchum & Walton.

Data centers’ electricity demand in 2030 will increase to nearly 12% of total U.S. annual consumption, according to the Environmental and Energy Study Institute, a Washington, D.C.-based nonprofit organization. Data center electricity consumption is stressing power grids in the U.S.

Broadcom

Broadcom has partnered with Mikros for its 3.5D eXtreme Dimension SiP (XDSiP), a stacked-die platform that combines 2.5D and 3D integration, enabling AI customers to develop custom accelerators (XPUs) and compute ASICs.

“Broadcom is designing the next-gen XPUs that are going to help the inference community over the next few years, and they look to us to be a driving thermal partner to dissipate the heat on these incredibly high-powered chips,” Matter told EE Times.

Mikros, acquired by Jabil in 2025, did designs for the International Space Station 30 years ago to cool high-power-density electronics. The company is adopting those designs for high-performance computing and AI.

“By partnering with Mikros and Jabil, we are ensuring our custom 5-kW XPU customers have access to a robust ecosystem of high-performance cooling,” Ken Kutzler, VP of AI systems development at Broadcom, said in an April announcement. “Their microchannel technology provides the critical chip-level thermal resistance necessary to unlock the full performance of our ASICs, enabling a seamless chip-to-chiller liquid cooling path that is essential for the next generation of high-density AI data centers.”

Liquid cooling

Upcoming data centers are adopting liquid cooling instead of less efficient air cooling, Matter said. Liquid cooling can help cut more than a third of the energy consumption from air-cooled data centers, which is spent running fans and air conditioning equipment instead of compute.

“Chips that are coming out in the next three years and they’re being designed for liquid,” he noted. “Liquid cooling is going to be fully the norm in probably three to five years. We are looking at savings with liquid cooling that could easily be upwards of a million dollars a rack over the lifetime of the rack.”

The company’s MikroMatrix design allows its cold plates to distribute coolant to match the power maps of next-generation GPUs.

“It also allows us to have very even cooling over the chip, and because we have this ‘pixel’ design,” Matter said. “When you have a new GPU with multiple zones, we can actually tailor the pixels to match the zones, and that provides for even more efficiency.”

Marvell

Mikros is also working with Marvell Technology on custom designs for co-packaged optics chips. “They’re actually bringing the optics in toward the chip, and so it was actually a cold plate that cooled not only the chip itself but the optics around it in one solid cold plate,” Matter said. “Those transceivers in themselves don’t transmit the amount of heat that a CPU or GPU would, but when packaged all together and close to the GPU, they can actually be affected by the GPU’s heat, so you need to be cooling the switch chip.”

Transitioning to liquid cooling enables higher densities of racks in an AI data center, Marvell said of the Mikros technology in a blog last year.

The increased rack density is driven by the MikroMatrix platform, using a matrix array of microchannels oriented perpendicular to the chip surface, dramatically increasing the contact area within the cold plate for better heat dissipation, Marvell noted.

“When a client works with a Broadcom or a Marvell chip, for instance, we are able to work with that client for any customization that’s needed as well,” Matter added.

Carbice

Carbice founder Baratunde Cola has been shipping the company’s carbon nanotube products to satellite makers for about seven years. “Almost every single prime satellite company is a customer to some degree of our material,” he told EE Times. “When we got into compute, the best place to start was gamers.”

Last year, Carbice started supplying its carbon nanotube TIM to gaming system maker Cyberpower.

“We’ll ship over half a million CPUs this year,” Cola said.

The vertical structure of Carbice nanotubes helps to dissipate heat by increasing surface area for free convection by a factor greater than 10,000, according to Cola. What differentiates carbon nanotubes from other TIMs is the ability to keep the cold plate stuck to the chip, he added.

“It’s a mechanical solution,” Cola said. “Carbice looks like an array of curly fries standing up. Those curly fries can both compress and extend. Every chip that’s made changes curvature with temperature, because the chips are multi-material. They have a thermal-expansion mismatch built into the manufacturing. You have a single point of failure at the connection point of the cold plate to the chip. Just because of the viscosity and the stress profile—and you cannot escape it—there will be random throttling events, random failures.”

With heterogeneous integration, material stacks are constantly changing, and the curvature problem becomes even more pronounced, Cola noted.

“Our stuff rides the change in curvature without losing contact,” he said. “The product we make is a simple sticky note. It’s just a peel-and-stick product.”

Carbice is opening a new production facility in Atlanta near the Georgia Institute of Technology, where Cola has been a professor of thermal management. The facility will triple Carbice’s output from a year ago to supply contract manufacturing OEMs such as Jabil and Flex, according to Cola.

“Our stuff’s been running in the Georgia Tech data center,” Cola said. “Georgia Tech gets early Nvidia chips; there’s a partnership. We’ve been running on Nvidia chips in the Georgia Tech data center for three years, so we know the failure rates.”

TIMs range from greases, gap pads, and phase-change materials to nanotubes and graphite solutions.

“When we interact with a client like Broadcom, for instance, we’re going to be looking for a TIM that best matches that need,” Matter said. “Sometimes, we’ll have to choose from one family or another based on size or performance or tolerances.”

The carbon nanotube TIM from Carbice has strengths in its ability to adjust to chip changes over time, Matter said.

“We know that team very well,” Matter noted. “They have a good product, and they’re very useful in some applications. There are various applications where customers will ask us for a specific type of TIM. Sometimes it might be carbon nanotube-focused, others might have different desires for the way that their thermal interface works, depending on their system.”

data.title: When Arm Meets RISC-V: SiPearl, Semidynamics to Co-Develop Sovereign AI Platform
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

As AI models become a key driver of global competitiveness and AI hardware used for training and inference is increasingly treated as a strategic asset rather than a commodity, developing sovereign AI platforms has gained importance as a way to reduce reliance on foreign suppliers. Europe, still a relatively small player in chip design and systems, lacks a homegrown AI hardware stack, unlike the U.S. and China. That gap may begin to narrow as SiPearl and Semidynamics this month announced a partnership to build one of the first European sovereign rack-scale AI platforms.

SiPearl-Semidynamics AI platform: hardware

The sovereign AI platform jointly developed by SiPearl and Semidynamics is a multi-generation, multi-year effort aimed at developing a competitive European rack-scale AI platform based on technologies from both companies.

The platform will rely on SiPearl’s Arm Neoverse V2-based Rhea2, and subsequent generations of processors, to handle general-purpose and orchestration tasks, alongside Semidynamics’ RISC-V-based AI inference accelerators (which the company describes as GPU/AI inference ASICs) serving as the main compute engines.

SiPearl CEO Philippe Notton told EE Times that Rhea2 is still ‘in design’ and that development is expected to move significantly faster than Rhea1, as the company now has a mature engineering team, established design flows, and experience developing complex HPC processors. Yet, unlike Rhea1, Rhea2 will lack a complex memory subsystem featuring onboard HBM for bandwidth-hungry HPC applications (e.g., fluid dynamics compute) and another DDR5 memory pool for capacity-heavy workloads. Rhea2 will only rely on a DDR-type memory, which will simplify memory coherence with AI accelerators.

The accelerators will use Semidynamics’ existing IP with refinements, drawing on architectures derived from the company’s current Cervell NPUs with on-chip memory.

“[The accelerators] are based on our previous IP developments, with refinements,” David Harold, CMO of Semidynamics, told EE Times. “It is closer to a programmable AI inference accelerator than to a conventional graphics GPU. We use GPU [term] in the data center AI sense: a device that performs the heavy AI compute in the rack. The architecture combines RISC-V CPU control, vector, and tensor capability, and a differentiated memory subsystem. The aim is to support large-scale inference workloads, including large models and long contexts, without locking customers into a narrowly fixed-function accelerator.”

While the first-generation platform will combine SiPearl’s Rhea2 CPUs and Semidynamics’ RISC-V accelerators, later generations are expected to pursue further integrations at the chiplet level, echoing data center-grade heterogeneous multi-chiplet designs, such as AMD’s Instinct MI300A. However, given architectural differences between Semidynamics’ architecture and AMD’s approach, the result will be completely different.

“The second iteration is intended to move toward tighter chiplet-level integration, but we would not describe it as equivalent to a specific existing architecture such as AMD’s MI300A,” Harold said. “Semidynamics is using a chiplet-based approach rather than a giant monolithic die, and chip-to-chip connectivity uses UCIe. Certainly, tighter integration with SiPearl’s silicon is possible and is being contemplated. The broader memory model and package-level details will be disclosed later.”

Notton continued, “A challenge we face in Europe, and we want to be a significant player, is to ensure that our chiplets will work with [other] chiplets, which will be accelerator chiplets coming from Semidynamics or others. With Rhea2, you have a UCI interface, so it can work with various accelerators.”

One of the challenges with accelerator architectures that feature on-chip memory is enabling memory with CPUs. Such designs are optimized to keep data local to maximize bandwidth efficiency and minimize latency, whereas coherence requires constant synchronization, cache tracking, and remote visibility across CPUs and accelerators. Hence, coherence can simplify programming but also erodes some of the benefits of such architectures, since coherence traffic increases power consumption, latency, area overhead, and verification complexity. From our conversation with Notton, one of the key points is that the first-generation platform is expected to support full CPU-accelerator memory coherence. Notton explicitly confirmed this and suggested the same direction applies to later generations as well.

“This is part of the work we will be doing with SiPearl in the near future,” Harold said.

Truth to be told, even advanced heterogeneous systems from AMD and Nvidia typically use hybrid coherence models, where only certain memory regions are kept coherent while many local accelerator memories remain outside coherent domains. At this point, it is hard to tell how SiPearl and Semidynamics plan to enable coherence.

For now, Semidynamics is not disclosing the RISC-V instructions and data formats supported by its upcoming AI accelerators, though the company says the processors will be programmable using industry-standard tools such as Linux, GCC, and LLVM, rather than relying on a closed proprietary software ecosystem.

“We are not yet disclosing the full instruction and data-format support,” Harold said. “At the architectural level, the point of using RISC-V is to preserve openness and programmability. The system is designed to work with standard software infrastructure such as Linux, GCC, and LLVM, rather than requiring customers to adopt a closed proprietary programming environment. We will disclose more on specific instructions and data formats closer to product introduction.”

The design will follow Open Compute Project (OCP) specifications to ensure compatibility with established cloud and data center ecosystems, as well as already available hardware. This approach is intended to speed up time to market and benefit from economies of scale across widely deployed components, ranging from solid-state drives to server racks, and from motherboard components to power supplies.

“Our intent is to reduce deployment friction by aligning with data center infrastructure practices and familiar software environments,” Harold said. “We are designing the platform for rack-scale deployment by operators and integrators, rather than as a standalone chip that requires customers to build everything around it. We will provide more detail on the specific OCP-compatible elements as the system design is disclosed.”

Rack-scale approach

In line with broader industry trends, SiPearl and Semidynamics are developing rack-scale platforms rather than just a CPU and just an accelerator that are meant to work together. Specifically, the companies expect the platform to offer ‘excellent performance‑per‑watt’ as well as to offer ‘density on par with leading AI platforms.’ Both statements are intentionally vague and avoid specific numbers, though the general message about building an efficient and competitive rack-scale platform is clear. “We are targeting data center rack-scale AI inference, not an edge or low-power-only PCIe product,” Harold said. “The roadmap includes a PCIe card for developers and early system work and extends to liquid-cooled data center rack solutions.”

The reference to liquid-cooled racks clearly points to a platform resembling those developed by Nvidia—and AMD—where an entire rack packs dozens of accelerators connected in scale-up configuration and functioning like a single accelerator. So, when Semidynamics and SiPearl mention density expected from leading global AI platforms, they almost certainly mean very high accelerator counts per rack. Yet, the claim about similar density does not necessarily transform into similar performance density.

“We are not yet disclosing the number of accelerators per rack, so it would be premature to map the design directly onto 72- or 144-accelerator configurations,” Harold said.

Rack-scale interconnection technologies, such as Nvidia’s NVLink, and their topology are key to unlocking the scalability of AI accelerators. While the interconnection fabric for the Semidynamics accelerators is certainly a thing that the two companies are working on, for now, they are not ready to discuss details about the technology.

“We are not disclosing the full rack-level fabric yet,” Harold said. “Semidynamics has developed a scale-up approach for connecting accelerators within the rack, and the chiplet-level connectivity uses UCIe. We will provide more detail on the CPU-accelerator interconnect and rack-level fabric as the platform moves closer to market.”

In any case, both next-generation SiPearl CPUs and the multi-chiplet-based AI solutions jointly developed by SiPearl and Semidynamics will use UCIe interconnects.

“For the next generations [of CPUs], we are moving to chiplet,” Notton said. “In fact, that is what big players [such as] Intel, AMD, and Nvidia are doing. We do not have any choice because it becomes quite [hard] to develop large dies. So we have to fragment the design. […] Once we have chiplets, it is mix and match. You can combo other stuff together.”

Notton also highlighted one of the industry’s biggest unresolved problems: ensuring reliable interoperability between chiplets from different vendors. He noted that current chiplet ecosystems from companies such as Intel, AMD, and Samsung Electronics remain largely vertically integrated, underscoring the importance of close collaboration between SiPearl and Semidynamics to ensure their multi-chiplet AI platform operates reliably.

Notton also explicitly confirmed that the platform will require a scale-up fabric conceptually similar to NVLink, though he declined to disclose implementation details. When asked directly about rack-scale fabrics, he acknowledged the importance of stitching the system together but said the topic is at an early stage.

“You still need to stitch all of them, all of those things together,” Notton said. “To stitch all of the things together, you need a scale-up fabric like NVLink, [but] we do not comment yet. […] It is quite early stage and sensitive.”

Software

A software ecosystem tailored for AI workloads is one of the primary reasons Nvidia remains the leading supplier of AI hardware. To compete not only with Nvidia, but with a host of other suppliers, SiPearl and Semidynamics need to ensure that their software stack is both accessible and flexible. The good news is that Arm-based CPUs for AI systems and RISC-V-based AI accelerators have already made it to the market and are already supported by a broad software ecosystem.

“The platform is intended to integrate with existing AI data center software environments rather than require customers to adopt a closed proprietary stack,” Harold said.

“Semidynamics expects the racks to support vLLM, SGL, ONNX, and PyTorch when they ship,” he added. “At the lower level, the company is emphasizing Linux, GCC, and LLVM support, using RISC-V openness to make the platform programmable and accessible to a broad software ecosystem. There will, of course, be Semidynamics software at the lower layers where PyTorch operators are dispatched to the accelerator, but the intent is to publish and open that layer rather than treat it as a closed CUDA-like island.”

Positioning

SiPearl and Semidynamics position their upcoming platform for public and private AI data center deployments. Specifically, they mention both enterprise-scale inference as well as large-scale AI clusters used to run performance-hungry large language models (LLMs) and retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) workloads. “The first-generation platform is focused on large-scale inference,” Harold said. “That includes LLM serving, large models, long-context workloads, and emerging workloads that require much larger context windows and datasets. We see inference moving beyond simple single-prompt LLM serving toward more demanding reasoning and multimodal use cases, including video and long-context processing. We are not making a separate product claim around agentic AI at this stage, but we note agentic AI puts additional stress on the memory capacity of AI agents, and the architecture is intended to support the direction inference and agentic workloads are moving.”

Speaking about agentic AI workloads, SiPearl’s CEO also suggested the industry is moving away from the traditional 1:8 CPU-to-GPU ratio toward something closer to 1:2 for many inference deployments. He also noted that agentic AI could drive demand for CPU-heavy or even pure CPU racks, which is something that executives from both Intel and Nvidia envision.

“What we see is [that] the CPU-to-GPU ratio is changing,” Notton said. “It was 1:8 in the past, […] 1:2 is a good target. […] For agentic AI, [we] even got requests of only CPUs, pure rack of CPUs.”

To address CPU-heavy workloads, the rack-scale solution will support the option of CPU-only racks, Notton said. Meanwhile, he stressed that the two companies are developing a reference design for partners—who have not yet been disclosed—to manufacture that their partners (which are not disclosed at this time) will produce, rather than selling complete racks themselves, unlike Nvidia. Still, Notton acknowledged that modern CPU vendors increasingly need to engage in rack-level design to ensure competitive time-to-market and appropriate system integration.

“The key is to ensure that we have a rack which is modular,” Notton told EE Times. “So, depending on the request, we can change the configuration. […] It is clear that we need ODMs to help us in building these kinds of platforms because we do not have any intention to ship and to design our own rack.”

Since the two companies are developing an AI platform that is aimed primarily at inference, their general focus is maximum efficiency, programmability, and low total cost of ownership rather than ultimate performance.

“We are not trying to win only on peak compute numbers,” Harold said. “We are designing for efficient token generation in real data center deployments. That means memory efficiency, programmability, system integration, and cost structure are as important as raw throughput. It is also why we are not dictating the use of HBM in the current design. HBM creates supply and cost challenges, and we believe there is value in a fundamentally different cost structure for inference systems.”

The companies’ reference to OCP compatibility suggests scalability from traditional enterprise deployments to hyperscale-class power infrastructure, although the latter is not mentioned specifically. Furthermore, both companies participate in the European Processor Initiative (EPI), EuroHPC, and some other government-led initiatives, so expect them to address government-backed infrastructure first, then move on to enterprises, and then approach hyperscalers. Still, the actual business plan is something that remains to be seen.

The platform also targets various European initiatives, such as AI Factory and Giga Factory programs, which favor domestic architectures and localized supply chains. Still, the companies acknowledge that the chips powering the platform will not be manufactured in Europe.

“Our aim is to give European AI infrastructure providers a credible European-led alternative for large-scale inference,” Harold explained. “That does not mean every element of the semiconductor supply chain is physically located in Europe. Advanced semiconductor manufacturing remains global. But Semidynamics brings European control over crucial architecture, system design, software direction, and commercial deployment, which are critical parts of sovereignty in AI infrastructure.”

On sovereignty, Notton offered unusually candid remarks about Europe’s semiconductor limitations. He said that for security reasons, design infrastructure, compute farms, and even emulation systems are kept in Europe, while advanced manufacturing and packaging remain dependent on Taiwan for now.

“What we do is design in Europe,” Notton said. “The compute farm to manage the design is in Europe and is European. In fact, it is our own compute farm. Our emulation capacity is in Europe, and we own it. […] Everything is done out of [the] U.S. cloud for safety reasons. Today, for generation 1 and generation 2, foundry packaging is in Asia. It is in Taiwan. No choice.”

He also identified advanced packaging as a potential first step toward restoring parts of semiconductor manufacturing capacity in Europe, citing Singapore-based Silicon Box and its Italian expansion in 2024.

“Advanced packaging may be the first one to arrive in Europe,” Notton said. “One of the good examples is Silicon Box in Italy. This company from Singapore has set up a base camp in Italy. It is maybe the first step to have some part of the fab in Europe.”

Availability

On availability, there is no firm launch timeline. Before introducing the Rhea2 CPU, SiPearl must first bring the Rhea1 processor to market; it is currently in sampling. As a result, the first-generation SiPearl-Semidynamics AI platform is likely still a few years away.

As for Semidynamics, the company has already completed a 3-nm test chip for architectural validation and customer evaluation, with a production design tape-out scheduled for later this year.

“We have completed a successful 3-nm test chip, which provides a platform for architectural validation and customer analysis,” Harold said. “A production tape-out is planned for later this year, followed by system bring-up. The next major milestones are production tape-out, silicon bring-up, system integration, and then customer deployment. We will be more specific on availability once those milestones are further advanced.”

Philippe Notton confirmed that the jointly developed SiPearl-Semidynamics AI platform is intended to arrive ‘this decade,’ not the next one. The final version of Rhea2 is scheduled to tape out in 2027, when hardware and software developers will begin system integration. He also stressed that SiPearl intends to dramatically shorten development cycles compared to Rhea1 and ultimately match the execution pace of major U.S. companies.

“It has to be this decade, that is why we need to act fast,” Notton said. “Rhea2 will tape out in 2027, let us say for the advanced version, but there is some other stuff that will happen before. […] Generation 1 took a while to be done because we had to build the company, we had to find the money. From generation 2 and generation 3, we definitely need to reduce the design cycles. [It] cannot take five years to design a processor. […] We need to be faster. Now the team is mature, we know what to do.”

data.title: Scaling Down Is the New Scaling Up
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

SANTA CLARA, Calif. — At the Embedded Vision Summit, keynote speaker Vikas Chandra, senior director at Meta Reality Labs, presented recent work by Meta that aims to make advanced perception and agentic AI available on personal devices and wearables, despite the limited hardware resources available.

“What if we could take a look at all the devices we have at our disposal: laptops, tablets, wearables, watches, phones, and so on, and enable them to be more intelligent?” Chandra said. “The question is no longer how big can the model get? The question becomes how much capability can we enable in these devices to make them intelligent?”

Chandra’s thesis is that we will move away from chatbots—not completely—in favor of personal agents that are always present, always private, and always ready. Our wearables and smartphones already know the context of our day-to-day lives, as well as timely context such as location, weather, and even health data like heart rate.  

However, running advanced multi-modal perception on a smartphone or a pair of glasses is a significant technical challenge; an agent would have to maintain persistent context despite operating within strict compute, memory, and power budgets. The most critical limit today is memory bandwidth, Chandra said.

“The most consequential work in the next five to 10 years [will not be] about how big can you make these models,” Chandra said. “It’s more about an AI that has a lot more context about you, so it is in some way able to help you on a day-to-day basis.”

Designing capable models that can work well within smartphone hardware limits will require four breakthroughs: quantization (shrinking the weights), architecture (shape of the model), runtime optimization (reducing wasted work), and vision (efficient multimodal perception). Chandra detailed recent Meta work in all four areas.

Quantization

For an on-device model, quantization is existential, Chandra said.

ParetoQ, published at NeurIPS 2025, showed Pareto curves for accuracy versus quantization for different types of models. The result was that for a fixed memory budget, bigger models (more parameters) at lower precision are more accurate than smaller models at higher precision.

Another interesting finding was that below 2 or 3 bits, the representation changes—that is, the way models learn below the 2- or 3-bit threshold is different. While Meta’s team is still working out why this happens, it was nonetheless interesting, Chandra said.

Outliers are a big problem for extreme quantization, since they are badly affected by the reduction in precision. Some techniques can “smoothen” the matrices, that is, they multiply activations with a diagonal or orthogonal smoothing matrix and apply the inverse to the weights, preserving mathematical equivalence while removing outliers. SpinQuant, published at the 2025 International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2025), learned the smoothening as part of the training process. This allowed the team to quantize below 4 bits without losing accuracy, Chandra said.

Architecture

It’s also possible to optimize the shape of a model for on-device use, Chandra said. In this context, “shape” means the size of the layers versus the number of layers.

In MobileLLM, presented at the 2024 International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2024), the team discovered that for a fixed memory budget or the same number of parameters, a tall and narrow architecture—smaller layers, more layers—worked better than wide and shallow. The team also used other tricks, such as block-wise weight sharing to reduce parameter counts, and embedding sharing between input and output.

The result was a family of sub-1B models that performed well on specific tasks they were tuned for.

“We moved away from 8B or 16B models to a 300 million parameter model, which is very, very tiny,” Chandra said. “That in itself was a huge, huge deal.”

Recent work (MobileLLM-Flash, presented at the 2026 Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics) has scaled the same model to 1B and shown it beats comparable-sized models on various benchmarks. Increasing the context window and finetuning produced sufficient generalization to make the model suitable for real use cases.

Meta has also experimented with hardware-in-the-loop training—this uses a loss function on every forward pass to simulate the results for specific hardware, so it can be optimized for latency. The technique reduced overall latency by half.

The team also created a reasoning model based on this work—MobileLLM-R1, presented at ICLR 2026.

This work breaks the assumption that serious reasoning requires massive cloud models, Chandra said.

Runtime optimization

“How do you make the agent feel faster?” Chandra said. “One of our expectations is… we don’t want the agent to take a long time to answer our questions, that would completely break the agentic behavior. […] We need to make these agents really fast so it feels like true human communication with the agent.”

Transformer networks are autoregressive, that is, they generate tokens one at a time based on the tokens that came before. This quality makes them difficult to parallelize.

One popular technique often used in “big AI” is speculative decoding.

“The idea is very, very simple,” Chandra said. “Instead of having one large model that spends a lot of its cycle to generate one correct next token, what if you have a lot of smaller models or graph models that are able to generate a whole bunch of tokens at the same time? Then you have a target model which can verify all of these tokens at the same time. Tokens that don’t fall into the solution are discarded.”

The smaller models can generate tokens fairly cheaply, and parallelizing in this way makes token generation more efficient, even though more steps are added. This technique can reduce latency by a factor of two or three, Chandra said, which is essential to making on-device agents feel truly responsive.

Vision models

“Vision is the most expensive modality in the sensing that we do,” Chandra said. “Models need to hear and see, and not in a disparate modality, but in a fused way, to understand the environment and act in real time.”

The team took Meta’s SAM (segment anything model) foundation model and refined it to EfficientSAM, a version with a less resource-heavy vision encoder, then compressed and distilled to make SqueezeSAM. Further optimizations produced EdgeTAM for segmentation and tracking across multiple frames of a video on smartphone hardware. It can run at 16fps on an iPhone 15 Pro Max, Chandra said.

“Video as a modality is extremely expensive,” he said. “Imagine if you could process an hour-long video with full context, that would be awesome, but at 30 or 60 fps, that’s a lot of frames, and it consumes a lot of tokens.”

The team discovered that many of the frames in a given video weren’t needed because they didn’t add new information. New work, LongVU, presented at ICML 2025, reduced token cost by an order of magnitude using this technique, thereby enabling video understanding on edge-class processors.

The team also developed a model, VideoAuto-R1, presented at the 2026 Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR 2026), which reuses reasoning tokens for follow-up questions on the same video.

“Reasoning traces you generate once can be reused over and over again for querying similar, or even disparate, sets of questions [on the same video],” Chandra said. “You don’t need to do reasoning again and again for each question.”

Another new model, DepthLM, is a vision-language model that can estimate how far away objects are based on simple 2D camera pictures. Understanding 3D space opens up many use cases in physical AI, Chandra said. 

The native on-device agent

Putting all these techniques together can enable on-device agents.

“An agent that sits on the devices you carry starts to behave more like an orchestrator,” he said. “It has an understanding of everything you see and perceive, and it should be able to proactively tell you things you need at that particular moment.”

The key thing is that models have to be built from the ground up for the devices they will run on, Chandra said, which means they have to start from the hardware constraints and design upwards.

“All of these things put together ultimately build a picture of who you are, what you like, what you do,” Chandra said. “It’s not going to be a chatbot that we interact with on our browser, and it won’t be the biggest model that wins. It will be a smart, efficient, distributed model that ultimately will build that story for the next decade.”

data.title: ADI to Acquire IVR Tech to Join Data Center’s Power Gold Rush
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The AI bandwagon has reached the power semiconductors’ doorstep, and that’s apparent from the Bloomberg report about Analog Devices Inc. (ADI) acquiring a startup specializing in integrated voltage regulators (IVRs) placed directly inside the processor package instead of being positioned beside the processor on the board.

ADI is reportedly in advanced talks to acquire the privately-owned power management IC (PMIC) startup Empower Semiconductor for $1.5 billion, though both companies initially declined to comment on the news. Empower’s IVRs claim to deliver on-demand, scalable power with the speed, precision, and signal integrity required by AI processors.

It’s a digitally configurable hardware platform that simplifies the adoption of DC/DC converters by having multiple power supplies in a single IC. While designers can simply place the chip on the PCB with no discrete components, the die can also be integrated into an AI accelerator package because it’s tiny and can be placed on the underside of the processor substrate.

Empower, co-founded in 2014 by three analog design veterans, saw data centers as a low-hanging fruit for the company’s new power chip, an integrated voltage regulator (IVR), which either eliminates or integrates the discrete components to ease the years-long balancing act between power density and energy efficiency.

In a typical PMIC solution, several discrete components make it slow, expensive, and large. That’s a problem because data centers are already reaching the limits of power consumption. Empower claims its power device replaces discrete-based, bulky PMICs while significantly reducing power loss and improving transient response.

Empower’s IVRs eliminate all discrete components and make the chip configurable and programmable in a tiny form factor that can be placed anywhere in the system. IVRs accomplish this by eliminating magnetic components and multi-layer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs). As a result, the entire package becomes three to five times smaller than an inductor used in a typical power delivery system.

Data center’s power bottleneck

Empower, despite this power regulation solution for powering AI processors, isn’t alone in this pursuit of vertical or power-on-package designs for modern AI compute infrastructure. Power semiconductor suppliers such as Infineon, Monolithic Power Systems (MPS), and Vicor are also making significant inroads with design wins in hyperscale environments.

Infineon has recently incorporated d-Matrix’s Corsair inference accelerator in its OptiMOS TDM2254xx dual-phase power modules to deliver sub-2 ms token latency required by interactive applications. Both MPS and Vicor are also making significant progress in providing cost-effective power management solutions for AI accelerators.

ADI, while having accumulated conventional power rail and module assets through the acquisitions of Linear Technology and Maxim Integrated, falls short in power delivery solutions for GPUs and other AI accelerators. So, instead of building a power delivery architecture from scratch, it decided to buy one with a track record of reducing data center energy footprints and total cost of ownership.

The Milpitas, California-based Empower has addressed a critical bottleneck in modern AI computing with a power management architecture that shrinks solution footprint, height, and component count. The news about its acquisition marks a triumph of the company’s laser focus on replacing board-level VMRs with a device co-packaged with SoC.

The news about ADI acquiring Empower is also a stark reminder that power semiconductors are now as integral to AI accelerator performance as advanced packaging and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) technologies. No wonder Empower’s senior managers called data centers a low-hanging fruit for their power-on-package solution.
